kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:23-24 — "The Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread: And when he had given thanks, he brake it."

kids.question: What does the bread represent in the Lord's Supper?
kids.option_a: The body of Jesus, which was broken for us
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:24 — "Take, eat: this is my body, which is broken for you: this do in remembrance of me."

kids.question: What does the cup represent?
kids.option_a: The new testament (covenant) in Jesus' blood
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:25 — "This cup is the new testament in my blood: this do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me."

teens.question: What problem was happening when the Corinthians gathered for the Lord's Supper?
teens.option_a: Some were eating all the food and getting drunk while others went hungry — they were not discerning the body of the Lord
teens.option_c: They came at different times by accident
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:21 — "For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken."

teens.question: What does Paul say about eating the bread and drinking the cup unworthily?
teens.option_a: He who eats and drinks unworthily is guilty of the body and blood of the Lord
teens.option_b: It makes no difference how you approach it
teens.option_c: Only the pastor can eat unworthily
teens.option_d: Unworthiness does not apply to believers
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:27 — "Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord."

teens.question: What should a person do before eating the Lord's Supper?
teens.option_a: Examine himself — let a man examine himself and so let him eat
teens.option_b: Just eat as quickly as possible
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:28 — "But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread, and drink of that cup."

teens.question: What consequences did some Corinthians experience because of their unworthy approach?
teens.option_a: Many were weak and sickly, and many had died ("sleep")
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:30 — "For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep."

teens.question: What did Paul tell the Corinthians to do when they come together to eat?
teens.option_a: Wait for one another — tarry one for another
teens.option_b: Start eating as soon as you arrive
teens.option_c: Eat at home and skip the church meal
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:33 — "Wherefore, my brethren, when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another."

adults.question: What is the order of headship Paul presents and what does "head" mean in context?
adults.option_a: God is the head of Christ, Christ is the head of every man, and the man is the head of the woman — "head" (kephalē) likely denotes source/authority, establishing a relational order without implying inferiority
adults.option_b: Women are inferior to men
adults.option_c: There is no order at all
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:3 — "But I would have you know, that the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man; and the head of Christ is God." The Son is not inferior to the Father despite being "headed" by Him, showing headship is relational, not about worth.

adults.question: What is the earliest written account of the Lord's Supper institution, and why is it significant?
adults.option_a: Paul's account here (written c. AD 55) predates the Gospel accounts — he received it "of the Lord" (direct revelation or authoritative tradition traceable to Jesus), making it the oldest record of the institution
adults.option_b: Matthew's account is earlier
adults.option_c: Luke wrote first
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:23 — "For I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you." Written around AD 55, this predates Mark (c. AD 65) and is the earliest extant written account of the Lord's Supper institution.

adults.question: What does "not discerning the Lord's body" mean in verse 29?
adults.option_a: Failing to recognise both the sacred significance of the elements (Christ's sacrificial body) and the corporate reality of the church as Christ's body — treating the Supper as a common meal while despising fellow believers
adults.option_b: Failing to see a physical transformation of the bread
adults.option_c: Not understanding nutrition
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:29 — "For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body." The double referent (sacramental body and ecclesiastical body) fits the context of both communion and community abuse.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world"?
adults.option_a: God's temporal discipline of believers (sickness, death) is corrective, not condemnatory — it is fatherly chastening to prevent final condemnation alongside the unbelieving world
adults.option_b: Believers can lose their salvation
adults.option_c: God never disciplines believers
adults.option_d: Discipline and condemnation are the same
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:32 — "But when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world." This distinguishes divine discipline (paideúō — parental training) from final judgment (katakrinō — condemnation).

adults.question: How does Paul's phrase "this do in remembrance of me" (anamnēsis) differ from mere recollection?
adults.option_a: Anamnēsis is active re-presentation — not merely recalling a past event but making its reality present and effective in the assembly, proclaiming the Lord's death until He comes
adults.option_b: It is simple nostalgia
adults.option_c: It has no theological significance
adults.option_d: It means performing a magic ritual
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:24-25 — "This do in remembrance of me." The Greek anamnēsis carries the Jewish Passover sense of active memorial — each celebration participates in and proclaims the redemptive event.

adults.question: What does "ye do shew the Lord's death till he come" reveal about the Lord's Supper?
adults.option_a: It is simultaneously backward-looking (proclamation of Christ's death), present (communion with the living Lord), and forward-looking (anticipating His return) — a comprehensive eschatological act
adults.option_b: It is only about the past
adults.option_c: It has no future dimension
adults.option_d: It is purely symbolic with no real significance
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:26 — "For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he come." The three temporal dimensions make the Supper a microcosm of redemptive history.

adults.question: What social abuse was occurring at the Corinthian Lord's Supper?
adults.option_a: Wealthy members were eating lavish private meals while poor members went hungry — creating class divisions that contradicted the unity symbolised by the one bread and one cup
adults.option_b: The food was prepared incorrectly
adults.option_c: The meeting place was too small
adults.option_d: There was a shortage of bread
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:21-22 — "For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken... despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not?" The Corinthian agape feast had become a mirror of Roman social stratification.

adults.question: What does "this cup is the new testament in my blood" establish theologically?
adults.option_a: It inaugurates the new covenant prophesied in Jeremiah 31:31-34 — Christ's blood ratifies a new covenant replacing the old Sinaitic covenant, providing forgiveness and internalized knowledge of God
adults.option_b: It is a reference to the old covenant
adults.option_c: It creates a third covenant alongside the old and new
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:25 — "This cup is the new testament in my blood." The Greek diathēkē kainē echoes Jeremiah 31:31 (LXX), explicitly identifying the Lord's Supper with the fulfilment of the new covenant promise.

kids.question: Does every Christian get the same spiritual gift?
kids.option_a: No, the Spirit gives different gifts to different people
kids.option_b: Yes, everyone gets exactly the same gifts
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:4 — "Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit."

kids.question: What does Paul compare the church to?
kids.option_a: A human body with many different parts that all work together
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:12 — "For as the body is one, and hath many members, and all the members of that one body, being many, are one body: so also is Christ."

kids.question: Can any part of the body say it does not need the other parts?
kids.option_a: No, the eye cannot say to the hand "I have no need of thee" — every part is needed
kids.option_b: Yes, some parts are not needed
kids.option_d: The body can work with just one part
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:21 — "And the eye cannot say unto the hand, I have no need of thee: nor again the head to the feet, I have no need of you."

teens.question: What are some of the spiritual gifts Paul mentions?
teens.option_a: Wisdom, knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, discerning of spirits, tongues, interpretation of tongues
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:8-10 — Paul lists nine gifts: word of wisdom, word of knowledge, faith, gifts of healing, working of miracles, prophecy, discerning of spirits, divers kinds of tongues, and interpretation of tongues.

teens.question: Who decides which gifts each person receives?
teens.option_a: The Holy Spirit — dividing to every man severally as He will
teens.option_b: Each person chooses their own gift
teens.option_d: Gifts are earned through hard work
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:11 — "But all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man severally as he will."

teens.question: What does Paul say about the weaker or less honourable members of the body?
teens.option_a: They are necessary — God gives more abundant honour to the parts that lack it, so there should be no division in the body
teens.option_b: They are unimportant and can be removed
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:22-24 — "Nay, much more those members of the body, which seem to be more feeble, are necessary... God hath tempered the body together, having given more abundant honour to that part which lacked."

teens.question: What happens when one member of the body suffers?
teens.option_a: All the members suffer with it — and if one member is honoured, all the members rejoice with it
teens.option_d: The body ignores the suffering part
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:26 — "And whether one member suffer, all the members suffer with it; or one member be honoured, all the members rejoice with it."

teens.question: Does everyone have the same role in the church?
teens.option_a: No — God set some as apostles, prophets, teachers, miracles, healings, helps, governments, tongues — not all have every gift
teens.option_b: Yes, everyone has exactly the same role
teens.option_c: Roles do not matter in the church
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:28-30 — "And God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that miracles... Are all apostles? are all prophets? are all teachers?"

adults.question: What Trinitarian structure does Paul present in verses 4-6?
adults.option_a: Diversities of gifts but the same Spirit, diversities of administrations but the same Lord, diversities of operations but the same God — the Trinity is the source of all spiritual gifts, with each Person distinctly involved
adults.option_b: Only the Spirit gives gifts
adults.option_c: Only God the Father is mentioned
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:4-6 — "Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord. And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all." Spirit, Lord (Son), and God (Father) are distinctly named.

adults.question: What does "by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body" mean?
adults.option_a: The Holy Spirit's work at conversion incorporates every believer — whether Jew or Greek, bond or free — into the one body of Christ; this is Spirit baptism creating organic unity
adults.option_b: It refers only to water baptism
adults.option_c: It describes a second blessing experience
adults.option_d: It applies only to the original apostles
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:13 — "For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body, whether we be Jews or Gentiles, whether we be bond or free; and have been all made to drink into one Spirit."

adults.question: How does Paul's body analogy counter both inferiority and superiority complexes in the church?
adults.option_a: Against inferiority: the foot and ear are still part of the body even if they are not the hand or eye (vv.15-16). Against superiority: the eye cannot say to the hand "I have no need of thee" (v.21). Every member is essential and interdependent
adults.option_b: Only superior members matter
adults.option_c: Inferiority is a sign of weak faith
adults.option_d: The body analogy is purely decorative
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:15-21 — Paul addresses both the temptation of less prominent members to withdraw ("I am not of the body," v.16) and the temptation of prominent members to dismiss others ("I have no need of thee," v.21).

adults.question: What does Paul mean by God "tempering the body together" and giving "more abundant honour to that part which lacked"?
adults.option_a: God intentionally designed the church so that less visible and less esteemed members receive compensating honour, preventing schism and ensuring mutual care — divine design produces interdependence
adults.option_b: God favours prominent members
adults.option_c: Less honourable parts should be removed
adults.option_d: The church is a hierarchy of value
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:24-25 — "God hath tempered the body together, having given more abundant honour to that part which lacked: That there should be no schism in the body; but that the members should have the same care one for another."

adults.question: What is the significance of Paul's ranked list: "first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers"?
adults.option_a: It establishes a functional priority for foundational ministries — apostles and prophets lay the foundation (Ephesians 2:20), teachers build on it; this is a rank of function, not personal worth
adults.option_b: It is a hierarchy of personal importance
adults.option_c: Teachers are more important than apostles
adults.option_d: The list is random with no order
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:28 — "And God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers." The ordinal numbering (prōton, deuteron, triton) indicates intentional ranking of foundational roles.

adults.question: What rhetorical effect do Paul's seven rapid-fire questions in verses 29-30 produce?
adults.option_a: Each expects a "no" answer (using mē in Greek), proving that no single gift is universal — directly countering the Corinthian elevation of tongues as the mark of true spirituality
adults.option_b: They expect "yes" answers
adults.option_c: They are open-ended discussion prompts
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:29-30 — "Are all apostles? are all prophets?... do all speak with tongues? do all interpret?" The Greek particle mē expects negative answers, definitively ruling out any single gift as normative for all believers.

adults.question: What does "covet earnestly the best gifts" mean, and how does it connect to chapter 13?
adults.option_a: Paul urges them to desire the greater (more edifying) gifts — but then reveals a "more excellent way" (love) that transcends and governs all gifts; love is not itself a gift but the indispensable context for all gifts
adults.option_b: They should compete for the most spectacular gifts
adults.option_c: Love replaces all spiritual gifts
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:31 — "But covet earnestly the best gifts: and yet shew I unto you a more excellent way." The "best gifts" are those that edify the body (prophecy over tongues — 14:1-5), but all are worthless without love (ch.13).

adults.question: How does Paul's phrase "Now ye are the body of Christ, and members in particular" define ecclesiology?
adults.option_a: The church is not merely a human organisation but the actual body of Christ on earth — each individual believer is a particular, irreplaceable member with a God-assigned function in the organic whole
adults.option_b: The church is merely a social club
adults.option_c: Individual members have no significance
adults.option_d: The body of Christ is only a metaphor with no real meaning
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:27 — "Now ye are the body of Christ, and members in particular." The Greek melē ek merous means "members individually" — each person has a specific, divinely appointed role within the corporate body.

kids.question: What is the most important thing according to this chapter?
kids.option_a: Love (charity)
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:13 — "And now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity."

kids.question: What does love (charity) do and not do?
kids.option_a: Love is patient and kind — it does not envy or boast
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:4 — "Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up."

kids.question: Will love ever end?
kids.option_a: No, love never fails — it lasts forever
kids.option_b: Yes, love ends when you grow up
kids.option_c: Love only lasts for a little while
kids.option_d: Love stops when things get hard
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:8 — "Charity never faileth."

teens.question: What is Paul without love, even if he could speak in tongues of men and angels?
teens.option_a: A sounding brass or a tinkling cymbal — just empty noise
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:1 — "Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal."

teens.question: Even if you have enough faith to move mountains, what are you without love?
teens.option_a: Nothing — without charity, I am nothing
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:2 — "And though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing."

teens.question: What does Paul compare our current understanding to?
teens.option_a: Seeing through a glass darkly — we know in part now, but one day we will know fully
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:12 — "For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known."

teens.question: What three things abide (remain) forever?
teens.option_a: Faith, hope, and charity (love) — and the greatest is love
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:13 — "And now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity."

teens.question: What does love not keep a record of?
teens.option_a: Wrongs — love thinketh no evil and rejoiceth not in iniquity
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:5-6 — "Doth not behave itself unseemly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil; Rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth."

adults.question: How does chapter 13 function within the broader argument of chapters 12-14?
adults.option_a: It is the theological hinge — chapter 12 establishes the diversity of gifts, chapter 13 establishes love as the governing principle for all gifts, and chapter 14 applies this to the specific issue of tongues vs. prophecy
adults.option_b: It is an independent hymn unrelated to its context
adults.option_c: It replaces the teaching on spiritual gifts
adults.option_d: It is a later addition by another author
adults.explanation: Chapter 13 is not a standalone poem but the strategic centre of Paul's argument on spiritual gifts. It answers 12:31's "more excellent way" and prepares for 14:1's "Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts."

adults.question: What does Paul's list of love's fifteen attributes reveal about the Corinthian problems?
adults.option_a: Each attribute addresses a specific Corinthian failing — they were impatient, envious, boastful, puffed up, rude, self-seeking, easily provoked, keeping score, and rejoicing in others' failures
adults.option_b: The list is generic with no connection to Corinth
adults.option_c: Paul is describing his own virtues
adults.option_d: The list is borrowed from Greek philosophy
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:4-7 — Each attribute mirrors a Corinthian vice: "envieth not" (they envied gifts), "vaunteth not itself" (they boasted), "is not puffed up" (they were arrogant — 4:6,18,19; 5:2; 8:1), "seeketh not her own" (they were selfish — 10:24,33).

adults.question: What does "when that which is perfect is come" refer to?
adults.option_a: Most likely the eschatological consummation — Christ's return or the eternal state — when partial knowledge and gifts give way to complete, face-to-face knowledge of God
adults.option_b: The completion of the New Testament canon
adults.option_c: The maturity of the church
adults.option_d: The destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:10,12 — "But when that which is perfect is come, then that which is in part shall be done away... then face to face." The "face to face" language echoes Numbers 12:8 and suggests direct encounter with God, best fitting the eschaton.

adults.question: What is the significance of "I shall know even as also I am known"?
adults.option_a: In the eschaton, believers will know God with the same directness and intimacy with which God now knows them — not omniscience, but relational, unmediated knowledge replacing the current partial and indirect knowing
adults.option_b: Believers will become omniscient
adults.option_c: This refers only to self-knowledge
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:12 — "Then shall I know even as also I am known." The verb epiginōskō (full knowledge) contraces ginōskō (partial knowledge) — the asymmetry of God knowing us will be resolved by our full knowing of Him.

adults.question: Why is love "the greatest" even above faith and hope?
adults.option_a: Faith and hope are temporal — faith will give way to sight and hope to fulfillment — but love is eternal, reflecting the very nature of God who is love (1 John 4:8); love participates in God's own character
adults.option_b: Because Paul personally preferred love
adults.option_c: Because faith and hope are unimportant
adults.option_d: Because love is easier to practise
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:13 — "The greatest of these is charity." Faith becomes sight (2 Corinthians 5:7) and hope becomes reality (Romans 8:24) at Christ's return, but love endures because it is the essence of God's own nature.

adults.question: How does "love beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things" work grammatically?
adults.option_a: The four "all things" (panta) clauses form a chiasm (A-B-B'-A'): bearing/enduring frame the outer ring (patient endurance), while believing/hoping form the inner ring (positive trust) — love covers every dimension of human experience
adults.option_b: The phrases are random with no structure
adults.option_c: They are four separate commands
adults.option_d: Each phrase contradicts the one before it
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:7 — "Beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things." The structure moves from passive endurance to active trust and back to endurance, creating a comprehensive portrait of love's resilience.

adults.question: What does Paul's childhood metaphor ("When I was a child... when I became a man") illustrate?
adults.option_a: The current exercise of partial gifts (tongues, prophecy, knowledge) is to the eschatological fullness as childhood is to adulthood — present spiritual experiences are real but immature compared to what is coming
adults.option_b: Childhood is sinful and adulthood is righteous
adults.option_c: Spiritual gifts are childish and should be abandoned now
adults.option_d: Growing up means losing all spiritual experiences
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:11 — "When I was a child, I spake as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child: but when I became a man, I put away childish things." The analogy legitimises present gifts while relativising them in light of future glory.

adults.question: How does Paul's claim that giving one's body to be burned without love profits nothing challenge the Corinthian value system?
adults.option_a: Even the most extreme act of self-sacrifice — martyrdom — is spiritually worthless without love, demolishing the idea that spectacular spiritual experiences or heroic acts can substitute for genuine Christ-like love
adults.option_b: Martyrdom is always wrong
adults.option_c: Physical sacrifice automatically equals spiritual merit
adults.option_d: Paul discourages all forms of sacrifice
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:3 — "And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing." The ultimate sacrifices of possessions and life itself are rendered meaningless without love.

kids.question: Which is more helpful in church — speaking in tongues or prophecy?
kids.option_a: Prophecy, because it speaks to people in a language they can understand and helps them
kids.option_b: Tongues, because it sounds more impressive
kids.option_d: Both are equally helpful in every situation
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:3-4 — "But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort. He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself; but he that prophesieth edifieth the church."

kids.question: Should church services be confusing or orderly?
kids.option_a: Orderly — God is not the author of confusion, but of peace
kids.option_c: It does not matter how services are run
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:33 — "For God is not the author of confusion, but of peace, as in all churches of the saints."

kids.question: What should everything in church be done for?
kids.option_a: To build up and help others — let all things be done unto edifying
kids.option_b: To make one person look important
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:26 — "Let all things be done unto edifying."

teens.question: Why does Paul prefer prophecy over tongues in the gathered church?
teens.option_a: Because prophecy edifies the whole church while uninterpreted tongues only edify the speaker
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:4-5 — "He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself; but he that prophesieth edifieth the church. I would that ye all spake with tongues but rather that ye prophesied: for greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues, except he interpret."

teens.question: What does Paul say about speaking in tongues without an interpreter?
teens.option_a: The speaker should keep silent in the church and speak to himself and to God
teens.option_b: He should speak as loudly as possible
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:28 — "But if there be no interpreter, let him keep silence in the church; and let him speak to himself, and to God."

teens.question: How many people should speak in tongues at a gathering, and how?
teens.option_a: At most two or three, and by course (taking turns), with an interpreter
teens.option_b: As many as want, all at once
teens.option_d: Only the pastor may speak in tongues
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:27 — "If any man speak in an unknown tongue, let it be by two, or at the most by three, and that by course; and let one interpret."

teens.question: What does Paul say about understanding in worship?
teens.option_a: He wants to pray and sing with both the spirit and the understanding — using the mind as well as the spirit
teens.option_b: Understanding does not matter in worship
teens.option_d: The mind should be turned off during worship
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:15 — "I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also: I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also."

teens.question: What would an unbeliever think if everyone was speaking in tongues at once?
teens.option_a: They would think the church members were mad (insane)
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:23 — "If therefore the whole church be come together into one place, and all speak with tongues, and there come in those that are unlearned, or unbelievers, will they not say that ye are mad?"

adults.question: How does Paul define prophecy's threefold function?
adults.option_a: Edification (building up), exhortation (urging action), and comfort (consolation) — prophecy addresses the congregation intelligibly for their spiritual benefit
adults.option_b: Prediction of future events exclusively
adults.option_c: Political commentary
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:3 — "But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort." New Testament prophecy is primarily forth-telling (speaking God's truth to the congregation) rather than exclusively fore-telling.

adults.question: What does Paul's statement "I thank my God, I speak with tongues more than ye all" accomplish rhetorically?
adults.option_a: It establishes Paul's credibility on the topic — he cannot be accused of opposing tongues from ignorance or jealousy — before he then subordinates tongues to prophecy in the assembly
adults.option_b: He is boasting about his superiority
adults.option_c: He is exaggerating
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:18-19 — "I thank my God, I speak with tongues more than ye all: Yet in the church I had rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue."

adults.question: What Old Testament text does Paul cite regarding tongues, and how does he apply it?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 28:11-12 — God spoke to Israel through foreign-sounding languages (Assyrian invaders) as a sign of judgment; tongues similarly function as a sign for unbelievers, not for believers
adults.option_b: Psalm 150 about praise instruments
adults.option_c: Genesis 11 about the Tower of Babel
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:21 — "In the law it is written, With men of other tongues and other lips will I speak unto this people; and yet for all that will they not hear me, saith the Lord" (Isaiah 28:11-12). Paul applies this to show tongues are a sign of judgment toward unbelievers.

adults.question: What regulations does Paul establish for orderly worship?
adults.option_a: Tongues: two or three speakers maximum, in turn, with interpretation required. Prophecy: two or three speakers, with others judging (evaluating). If no interpreter, the tongue-speaker must be silent. All in order
adults.option_b: No regulations are needed — the Spirit will direct
adults.option_c: Only one person may ever speak
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:27-33 — Paul provides specific regulations: tongues (2-3, by course, with interpretation), prophecy (2-3, with evaluation), and the overarching principle that "God is not the author of confusion, but of peace" (v.33).

adults.question: What is the effect of prophecy on an unbeliever entering the assembly?
adults.option_a: The secrets of his heart are made manifest — he is convicted, judged, and falls down to worship God, declaring "God is in you of a truth"
adults.option_b: He would be impressed by the spectacle
adults.option_c: He would leave immediately
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:24-25 — "But if all prophesy, and there come in one that believeth not... he is convinced of all, he is judged of all: And thus are the secrets of his heart made manifest; and so falling down on his face he will worship God, and report that God is in you of a truth."

adults.question: What does "the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets" mean?
adults.option_a: Prophetic utterance is under the speaker's voluntary control — genuine inspiration does not produce ecstatic frenzy; a prophet can choose when to speak or be silent, maintaining order
adults.option_b: Prophets cannot control when they speak
adults.option_c: The Spirit forces uncontrollable utterances
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:32 — "And the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets." This distinguishes Christian prophecy from pagan ecstatic frenzy (manticism) where the speaker lost self-control. The Holy Spirit works with, not against, human volition.

adults.question: How does Paul's principle "let all things be done decently and in order" relate to Corinthian worship problems?
adults.option_a: The Corinthians' worship was chaotic — multiple tongue-speakers at once, no interpretation, everyone vying to participate simultaneously — Paul corrects this with the principle that divine worship should reflect God's character of order and peace
adults.option_b: Corinthian worship was already perfectly ordered
adults.option_c: Paul is being overly controlling
adults.option_d: Order is the opposite of spiritual freedom
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:40 — "Let all things be done decently and in order." The Greek euschēmonōs kai kata taxin means "with propriety and according to arrangement." This is Paul's final summary of the worship regulations in chapter 14.

adults.question: What does Paul mean by "Be not children in understanding: howbeit in malice be ye children, but in understanding be men"?
adults.option_a: The Corinthians were being childish in their pursuit of spectacular gifts like tongues — Paul urges them to be mature in their theological understanding while maintaining childlike innocence regarding evil
adults.option_b: He wants them to be literally childish
adults.option_c: He is against all forms of maturity
adults.option_d: He wants them to be malicious adults
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:20 — "Brethren, be not children in understanding: howbeit in malice be ye children, but in understanding be men." Paul calls for intellectual and spiritual maturity in evaluating gifts, combined with moral innocence.

kids.question: Did Jesus rise from the dead?
kids.option_a: Yes! Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again the third day
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 — "Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures."

kids.question: Will believers also be raised from the dead?
kids.option_a: Yes, because Jesus rose, all who believe in Him will be raised too
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:20-22 — "But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept... even so in Christ shall all be made alive."

kids.question: What will happen to death in the end?
kids.option_a: Death will be swallowed up in victory — it will be completely defeated
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:54 — "Death is swallowed up in victory."

teens.question: Who are some of the people who saw Jesus after He rose?
teens.option_a: Cephas (Peter), then the twelve, then over 500 brethren at once, then James, then all the apostles, and lastly Paul himself
teens.option_c: No one saw Him after the resurrection
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:5-8 — "He was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve: After that, he was seen of above five hundred brethren at once... After that, he was seen of James; then of all the apostles. And last of all he was seen of me also."

teens.question: If Christ is not risen, what does Paul say about the Christian faith?
teens.option_a: Our faith is vain (empty), we are still in our sins, and we are of all men most miserable
teens.option_b: Our faith is still perfectly fine
teens.option_c: It does not matter whether He rose
teens.option_d: We can believe whatever we want
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:14,17,19 — "If Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain... ye are yet in your sins... we are of all men most miserable."

teens.question: What is the last enemy that will be destroyed?
teens.option_a: Death
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:26 — "The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death."

teens.question: How does Paul describe the resurrection body compared to our current body?
teens.option_a: Our current body is sown in corruption, dishonour, and weakness; it is raised in incorruption, glory, and power — a natural body is sown, a spiritual body is raised
teens.option_b: The resurrection body is exactly the same
teens.option_c: We will not have bodies at all
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:42-44 — "It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption: It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory: it is sown in weakness; it is raised in power: It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body."

teens.question: What triumphant words does Paul write about death at the end of the chapter?
teens.option_a: "O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?" — the sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law, but thanks be to God who gives us victory through Jesus Christ
teens.option_c: The grave has the final word
teens.option_d: There is no hope against death
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:55-57 — "O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory? The sting of death is sin; and the strength of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ."

adults.question: What creedal formula does Paul cite in verses 3-5, and why is it significant?
adults.option_a: "Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, was buried, rose again the third day according to the scriptures, and was seen" — this is a pre-Pauline creed dating to within years of the crucifixion, demonstrating very early belief in the resurrection
adults.option_b: Paul created this formula himself
adults.option_c: It was composed in the second century
adults.option_d: It is a later addition to the text
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:3-5 — "For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received." The verbs "received/delivered" (paralambano/paradidomi) are technical terms for transmitting authoritative tradition, indicating this is pre-Pauline creedal material.

adults.question: What does "firstfruits" (aparchē) mean when applied to Christ's resurrection?
adults.option_a: Christ's resurrection is the initial harvest guaranteeing the full harvest — just as the firstfruits offered in the temple represented and guaranteed the entire crop, Christ's rising guarantees believers' future resurrection
adults.option_b: Christ was the first person ever to die
adults.option_c: It refers to agricultural practices only
adults.option_d: It means Christ's resurrection was incomplete
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:20,23 — "But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept... Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ's at his coming." The Feast of Firstfruits (Leviticus 23:10) typologically pictures Christ's resurrection.

adults.question: How does Paul's Adam-Christ typology work in verses 21-22 and 45-49?
adults.option_a: The first Adam brought death to all through sin; the last Adam (Christ) brings resurrection life to all who are in Him. The first Adam was a living soul from the earth; the last Adam is a life-giving spirit from heaven — believers will bear the image of the heavenly
adults.option_b: Adam and Christ are the same person
adults.option_c: The comparison is purely metaphorical
adults.option_d: Paul invents this parallel with no OT basis
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:21-22,45 — "For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead... The first man Adam was made a living soul; the last Adam was made a quickening spirit." This is a federal headship argument — humanity's destiny is determined by its representative head.

adults.question: What does Paul teach about the nature of the resurrection body?
adults.option_a: It is continuous with yet transformed from the present body — sown/raised contrasts (corruption/incorruption, dishonour/glory, weakness/power, natural/spiritual) show both continuity (same body sown and raised) and radical transformation
adults.option_b: It is an entirely new body unrelated to the old
adults.option_c: Resurrection is purely spiritual with no body
adults.option_d: The resurrection body is identical to the current one
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:42-44 — The seed analogy (vv.36-38) establishes continuity (the same seed that is planted grows), while the contrasts establish transformation. The resurrection body is the present body glorified, not a replacement.

adults.question: What "mystery" does Paul reveal in verses 51-52?
adults.option_a: "We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed, in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump" — living believers at Christ's return will be instantaneously transformed without dying
adults.option_b: All believers must die before being resurrected
adults.option_c: The mystery is that resurrection will not happen
adults.option_d: Only the dead will be changed
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:51-52 — "Behold, I shew you a mystery; We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed, In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed."

adults.question: How does Paul's argument "if the dead rise not... let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die" function?
adults.option_a: It is a reductio ad absurdum — if there is no resurrection, then Christian morality, sacrifice, and suffering are pointless, and hedonism (quoting Isaiah 22:13) is the only logical response; but since this conclusion is absurd, the premise (no resurrection) must be false
adults.option_b: Paul is recommending hedonism
adults.option_c: He is quoting Greek philosophy approvingly
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:32 — "If after the manner of men I have fought with beasts at Ephesus, what advantageth it me, if the dead rise not? let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die." Paul shows the existential absurdity of denying resurrection.

adults.question: What does "flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God" mean?
adults.option_a: The present mortal, perishable body in its current state is unsuited for the eternal kingdom — transformation (not destruction) is required; the body must be clothed with incorruption and immortality
adults.option_b: Physical matter is inherently evil
adults.option_c: Bodies will be abolished entirely
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:50 — "Now this I say, brethren, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption." "Flesh and blood" is a Semitic idiom for mortal human nature, not a statement against materiality per se.

adults.question: What does "then cometh the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father" reveal about eschatology?
adults.option_a: Christ's mediatorial kingdom culminates when all enemies (including death) are subdued; He then delivers the kingdom to the Father and subjects Himself, so that "God may be all in all" — the Son's voluntary submission preserves Trinitarian unity
adults.option_b: Christ's kingdom replaces the Father's
adults.option_c: The Son ceases to exist
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:24,28 — "Then cometh the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father... then shall the Son also himself be subject unto him that put all things under him, that God may be all in all."

kids.question: What did Paul ask the Corinthians to collect for?
kids.option_a: A collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem
kids.option_b: A collection for building a palace
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:1 — "Now concerning the collection for the saints, as I have given order to the churches of Galatia, even so do ye."

kids.question: When did Paul say they should set aside their giving?
kids.option_a: On the first day of the week
kids.option_b: On the last day of the month
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:2 — "Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him."

kids.question: What does Paul tell them to do at the very end of the letter?
kids.option_a: "Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong. Let all your things be done with charity (love)"
kids.option_c: Give up when things get hard
kids.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:13-14 — "Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong. Let all your things be done with charity."

teens.question: How should each person decide how much to give?
teens.option_a: As God has prospered him — proportional giving based on what God has provided
teens.option_d: Only what they can spare after all expenses
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:2 — "Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him, that there be no gatherings when I come."

teens.question: How did Paul want them to treat Timothy when he visited?
teens.option_a: See that he is with you without fear and do not despise him — for he does the work of the Lord as Paul does
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:10-11 — "Now if Timotheus come, see that he may be with you without fear: for he worketh the work of the Lord, as I also do. Let no man therefore despise him."

teens.question: What did Paul say about Apollos visiting Corinth?
teens.option_a: Paul greatly desired Apollos to come, but Apollos was not willing to come at that time — he would come when he had a convenient time
teens.option_b: Apollos refused to ever visit Corinth
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:12 — "As touching our brother Apollos, I greatly desired him to come unto you with the brethren: but his will was not at all to come at this time; but he will come when he shall have convenient time."

teens.question: What household does Paul commend for their service?
teens.option_a: The household of Stephanas — they were the firstfruits of Achaia and had devoted themselves to the ministry of the saints
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:15 — "I beseech you, brethren, (ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and that they have addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints)."

teens.question: What Aramaic word does Paul use near the end of the letter?
teens.option_a: Maranatha — meaning "Our Lord, come!" or "The Lord is coming"
teens.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:22 — "If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha." Maranatha is an Aramaic word meaning "Our Lord, come!" — an early Christian prayer for Christ's return.

adults.question: What does Paul's instruction about the collection reveal about early church practice?
adults.option_a: Weekly, proportional, systematic giving on the first day of the week (Sunday) — indicating that Christians already gathered for worship on Sundays and that financial generosity was organised, not haphazard
adults.option_b: Giving was completely spontaneous
adults.option_c: Collections only happened once
adults.option_d: Sunday worship had not yet begun
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:1-2 — "Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him." This is early evidence for Sunday as the Christian day of assembly (cf. Acts 20:7, Revelation 1:10).

adults.question: What does Paul's reference to "a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries" reveal about his ministry philosophy?
adults.option_a: Effective ministry and opposition go hand in hand — Paul sees open doors and adversaries as simultaneous, not contradictory; opposition is not a reason to leave but rather confirms that significant work is happening
adults.option_b: Opposition means God has closed the door
adults.option_c: Paul is complaining about his difficulties
adults.option_d: He plans to avoid all opposition
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:9 — "For a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries." Paul was in Ephesus during this time (cf. Acts 19), where he experienced both tremendous gospel advance and intense opposition.

adults.question: What does the phrase "If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha" combine?
adults.option_a: A solemn curse (anathema — devoted to destruction/excommunication) with an eschatological prayer (Maranatha — "Our Lord, come!") — invoking divine judgment on those who do not love Christ while simultaneously praying for His return
adults.option_b: A simple farewell greeting
adults.option_c: A blessing for everyone
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:22 — "Anathema" is a Greek term for a curse of separation (cf. Romans 9:3; Galatians 1:8-9), while "Maranatha" is Aramaic — the juxtaposition of Greek curse and Aramaic prayer reflects the bilingual early church and its apocalyptic expectation.

adults.question: What does Paul's commendation of Stephanas's household as "firstfruits of Achaia" reveal?
adults.option_a: They were the first converts in the province of Achaia (southern Greece) — Paul uses agricultural firstfruits language (cf. Romans 16:5) to denote both chronological priority and representative significance
adults.option_b: They were the wealthiest family
adults.option_c: They produced the best crops
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:15 — "Ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia." Note that in Acts 17:34, Dionysius and Damaris believed in Athens (also in Achaia), but Stephanas was likely converted during Paul's earlier visit to Corinth (Acts 18:1-11).

adults.question: What does Paul's statement about Apollos — "his will was not at all to come at this time" — reveal about apostolic relationships?
adults.option_a: Apollos exercised independent judgment about his travel plans despite Paul's strong desire — showing that early church leaders were colleagues, not subordinates under a papal hierarchy; Apollos had genuine autonomy
adults.option_b: Paul controlled Apollos's schedule
adults.option_c: Apollos was rebelling against Paul
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:12 — "As touching our brother Apollos, I greatly desired him to come unto you with the brethren: but his will was not at all to come at this time." The Greek thelēma (will/desire) is emphatic — Apollos made his own decision, and Paul respected it.

adults.question: How do the five rapid imperatives in verses 13-14 function as a summary of the letter?
adults.option_a: "Watch" (against doctrinal error — ch.15), "stand fast in the faith" (against division — ch.1-4), "quit you like men" (against moral cowardice — ch.5-6), "be strong" (against self-indulgence — ch.8-10), "let all things be done with charity" (the governing principle — ch.13)
adults.option_b: They are random military commands
adults.option_c: They have no connection to the letter
adults.option_d: They are borrowed from Roman military manuals
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:13-14 — These five imperatives compress the entire letter's concerns into a memorable charge. "Quit you like men" (andrizesthe) means "act courageously/maturely" — the same call to maturity found throughout the letter.

adults.question: What is the significance of Paul writing the final greeting "with mine own hand"?
adults.option_a: Paul typically dictated his letters through an amanuensis (secretary) but wrote the final greeting personally as authentication — proving the letter's genuineness and adding a personal touch (cf. Galatians 6:11, Colossians 4:18, 2 Thessalonians 3:17)
adults.option_b: He could not afford a secretary
adults.option_c: The rest of the letter was not his
adults.option_d: He was testing a new pen
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16:21 — "The salutation of me Paul with mine own hand." This autographic practice was common in ancient letter-writing as a seal of authenticity, confirming that the dictated content was genuinely from the named author.

adults.question: How does 1 Corinthians 16 balance practical instructions with theological depth?
adults.option_a: Paul seamlessly weaves mundane logistics (travel plans, collections, household greetings) with profound theological content (anathema/Maranatha, standing in the faith, love as governing principle) — showing that practical church life and deep theology are inseparable
adults.option_b: The chapter is purely practical with no theology
adults.option_c: It is purely theological with no practical content
adults.option_d: It contradicts the rest of the letter
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 16 integrates Paul's travel plans (vv.5-9), personnel matters (vv.10-12,15-18), theological charge (vv.13-14), solemn warning (v.22), and personal greeting (vv.19-24) — modelling how gospel truth shapes every dimension of church life.
